title: Cedar and lavender wardrobe sachet
subtitle: Dried lavender, cedar shavings, and rosemary sewn into muslin bags to deter clothes moths
excerpt: Cedar and lavender are the two most effective traditional plant-based moth deterrents. Neither kills moths; they deter adult females from laying eggs in natural fibres. The scent must be present and refreshed regularly to remain effective. Sew them into small muslin bags and hang in the wardrobe or tuck into drawers.
sourceNotes: Cedar and lavender moth deterrent properties from textile conservation literature and natural pest management references.

recipe.batchNotes: Make as many sachets as needed. A wardrobe with wool, cashmere, or silk should have at least one sachet per shelf section.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 6 months of active deterrence before the scent fades. Refresh by adding 5 drops of cedar or lavender essential oil to each sachet and leaving to absorb for an hour. Replace the filling annually.

glossaryTerms.definition: A material or scent that discourages adult clothes moths (Tineola bisselliella) from laying eggs in natural fibres such as wool, cashmere, silk, and mohair. Cedar and lavender are the most effective traditional plant-based deterrents. They deter, not kill, and must be maintained as a continuous presence.

Aromatic wood shavings from the eastern red cedar (Juniperus virginiana). They contain thujaplicins and other volatile compounds that deter moths. The deterrent effect diminishes as the volatile oils evaporate; sanding the surface of a cedar block or adding cedar essential oil refreshes it.

Lavender (Lavandula angustifolia) contains linalool and other terpenoid compounds that clothes moths avoid. It is most effective in concentrated form in a small sachet placed directly with vulnerable garments.

A small bag made from loosely woven cotton muslin fabric. The open weave allows fragrance to pass through freely while containing the filling. Used for potpourri, herbs, and dried botanicals placed in drawers, wardrobes, and linen presses.

content.text: Clothes moths lay eggs in natural fibres, particularly those with traces of perspiration or food. The resulting larvae eat the fibre, leaving holes.
are the most traditional and well-documented plant-based

and placed directly with vulnerable garments, they create a scent barrier that deters egg-laying. Effectiveness lasts approximately 6 months; refresh by adding 5 drops of cedar or lavender essential oil to the sachet. Replace the filling annually. Shelf life is 6 months of active deterrence.

items.prepNote: 50% of filling; use English lavender for best fragrance
10% of filling; adds a complementary green note and additional deterrent properties

content.text: Combine lavender, cedar shavings, and rosemary in a bowl and mix gently. Fold each muslin square in half and sew two sides closed, leaving the top open. Fill each sachet with approximately a quarter of the mixture, leaving enough room to close the top. Sew or tie the top closed, securing a loop of ribbon as you do so for hanging.

Hang one sachet per section of the wardrobe, or tuck between folded garments in a drawer. Place them directly with the garments most at risk: wool jumpers, cashmere, silk blouses, and natural fibre blankets.

Active deterrence lasts approximately 6 months. Refresh by dropping 5 drops of cedar essential oil or lavender essential oil onto the outside of the sachet and allowing to absorb for 1 hour before returning to the wardrobe. Replace the filling once a year in spring, when clothes moths become active. Store unused sachets in a sealed tin to preserve the scent until needed.
